impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patent holder's unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the unpatented products must have substantial non-infringing uses to escape antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers agreeing to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products, and the court upheld the finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems) beyond the patent's scope. This is a significant ruling on the intersection of patent rights and antitrust law.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of patent misuse through tying arrangements can point to this case for guidance on the 'substantial non-infringing uses' defense—if the tied product truly lacks such uses, the patent holder may be protected.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who condition licensing on exclusive purchasing of unpatented products face significant antitrust exposure, including damages, where the tied products have uses beyond the patent's scope.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model of function calls are directed to an abstract idea under step one of the Alice framework, and whether damages for patent infringement may include foreign sales.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted claims of its cybersecurity patents were abstract under Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales. This is a major ruling narrowing patent eligibility for software-related security patents and limiting extraterritorial patent damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against software patent assertions gain a stronger basis to challenge patent eligibility under § 101, and the foreign sales ruling limits damages exposure to domestic activities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the cybersecurity and software space face heightened eligibility challenges, and universities and other licensors may recover significantly less in damages due to the exclusion of foreign sales revenue.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content presentation specifications to a wireless device's capabilities before delivering that content are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods of tailoring content delivery to device screen size and capabilities were ineligible for patenting under § 101, reversing a jury verdict of infringement and $2.5 million damages award. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content for a device's characteristics. This ruling reinforces strict Alice scrutiny for content delivery and streaming technology patents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies like Netflix gain stronger defenses against patent assertions covering routine content adaptation and delivery methods.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the content delivery and streaming space face greater risk that their claims will be found abstract, undermining the value of existing patent portfolios.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was proper on inequitable conduct in patent procurement and on a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on the same patent fraud.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. Tenaris allegedly failed to disclose prior CYMAX documents when prosecuting patents on its BlueCoil coiled tubing products. The court held that factual disputes about intent to deceive and materiality precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct, and similarly precluded summary judgment on Global Tubing's antitrust claim. This case highlights the intersection of patent enforcement and antitrust liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies facing patents obtained through potentially fraudulent means have a clearer path to pursue both inequitable conduct defenses and Walker Process antitrust counterclaims through trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who fail to disclose material prior art during prosecution face dual exposure to patent unenforceability and antitrust damages, including treble damages for attempted monopolization.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's ruling that the ITC's blanket practice of designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with 19 U.S.C. §§ 1516a and 1677f, and that the statute does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. This ruling could significantly increase transparency in trade remedy proceedings by requiring case-by-case confidentiality determinations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies participating in trade remedy proceedings as complainants or interested parties may gain greater access to information previously kept confidential, improving their ability to evaluate and challenge trade determinations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting business-sensitive information in ITC questionnaire responses face greater risk of public disclosure, potentially deterring candid responses or creating competitive harm.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing the exercise of discretion to decline to institute inter partes review proceedings constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions for exercising discretion to decline IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court emphasized that the Director retains ultimate authority over non-institution decisions and that Congress insulated such decisions from judicial review. This solidifies the PTO's broad discretion in managing IPR proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's retained flexibility to decline institution of IPR proceedings, providing some insulation of issued patents from post-grant challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners face continued uncertainty about whether IPR petitions will be instituted, with limited ability to challenge PTO discretionary policies through APA notice-and-comment or judicial review.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment for absence of a remedy based on that exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert and vacated summary judgment in Microsoft's favor. The court found the district court abused its discretion in excluding the expert report on patent damages. This ruling reinforces that trial courts must carefully apply Daubert standards to damages experts in patent cases and should not use exclusion of a damages expert as a basis to dispose of an entire infringement case on summary judgment.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs gain protection against overly aggressive Daubert challenges to damages experts that could effectively eliminate their case without reaching the merits of infringement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers like Microsoft face the prospect of trials on damages where expert testimony may be more liberally admitted, increasing potential exposure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding two expert witnesses on induced patent infringement and erred in granting JMOL of non-infringement of surgical device patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and its grant of JMOL to DePuy, remanding for a new trial on induced infringement of spinal surgery patents. The court found the exclusion of both expert witnesses was an abuse of discretion and that the JMOL was improper. This case is significant for the medical device industry regarding the evidentiary standards for proving induced infringement by device manufacturers through surgeon use of patented surgical techniques.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device space benefit from a more permissive standard for expert testimony supporting induced infringement claims based on physician use of patented methods.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face increased exposure to induced infringement claims, as courts may be less willing to exclude infringement experts and grant JMOL on such claims.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims reciting a 'control means' means-plus-function limitation for a crop harvester header control system are indefinite where the specification describes the corresponding structure as 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that the asserted claims of a crop harvester patent were invalid as indefinite, holding that the specification's description of the corresponding structure for the 'control means' limitation was sufficient. This ruling is significant for agricultural technology and broadly for means-plus-function claim drafting, clarifying when a specification adequately identifies corresponding structure.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from a more flexible standard for disclosing corresponding structure, particularly where the specification describes components as conventional.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies like Deere that relied on indefiniteness defenses face renewed infringement exposure, and the ruling may make it harder to invalidate means-plus-function claims for lack of structural disclosure.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a mandatory, money-mandating obligation entitling eligible applicants to damages under the Tucker Act when the SBA failed to award grants in the statutorily required order.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of Federal Claims' denial of the government's motion to dismiss, holding that over 300 restaurants stated viable Tucker Act claims for unpaid RRF grants. The court found that Congress's use of mandatory language ('shall award grants' in the order received) created an enforceable obligation, and the SBA's failure to process applications in the required order gives rise to a damages claim. This ruling opens the door for hundreds of restaurants to recover grant amounts they were entitled to receive.
impacts.positiveImplications: Restaurants and small businesses that were improperly denied RRF grants may recover significant damages from the government, and the ruling reinforces that Congress's mandatory grant language creates enforceable rights.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly entered a limited exclusion order rather than a general exclusion order against defaulting respondents in a Section 337 trademark investigation, and whether Crocs's appeal of the no-violation finding against active respondents was timely.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding against active respondents as untimely and affirmed the limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents. The ITC had found no likelihood of confusion or dilution of Crocs's 3D trade dress marks by active respondents but entered a limited exclusion order against defaulting parties under Section 337(g)(1). This case is significant for brand owners seeking to use ITC proceedings to protect trade dress and trademarks against imported goods.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and retailers benefit from the ITC's rigorous application of likelihood-of-confusion and dilution standards, limiting the ability of trade dress holders to obtain broad exclusion orders.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners seeking general exclusion orders at the ITC face challenges where active respondents contest the merits, and untimely appeals forfeit the ability to challenge adverse findings.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities enabling participants to asynchronously view data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal of a patent infringement action against Google, finding the web conferencing patent claims ineligible under § 101. The claims, which covered time-shifting and asynchronous viewing of multiple data streams in web conferences, were held to be directed to abstract ideas. This adds to the growing body of Federal Circuit authority invalidating software and internet-technology patents under Alice.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies operating web conferencing and streaming platforms benefit from the continued narrowing of patent eligibility for generic software functionality claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the web conferencing and collaboration technology space face increasing difficulty asserting patents with broad functional claims.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether substantial evidence supported the jury's findings of non-obviousness and damages for a patent covering pre-lit artificial trees with integrated mechanical and electrical connections.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's motions for JMOL of obviousness and for a new trial on damages, upholding the jury verdict in Willis's favor on its pre-lit artificial tree patent. The court found sufficient evidence supported both the non-obviousness finding and the damages award. This ruling is significant for consumer product companies, affirming that incremental improvements in product assembly technology can be patentable and enforceable.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with patents covering practical product improvements benefit from the Federal Circuit's deference to jury findings on non-obviousness and damages.
impacts.negativeImplications: Importers and competitors of patented consumer products face sustained enforcement risk and damages exposure where jury verdicts are upheld on appeal.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether registered nurses employed by the Indian Health Service under Title 5 are entitled to enhanced compensation rates for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under Title 38 provisions available to Title 5 healthcare employees.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Court of Federal Claims' dismissal of claims by IHS registered nurses seeking enhanced pay for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work during the COVID-19 pandemic. The court held that the nurses adequately stated claims under 38 U.S.C. § 7453 and applicable Title 5 regulations for pay enhancements that IHS had declined to apply. This ruling could affect compensation for thousands of federal healthcare workers and establish broader entitlement to enhanced pay provisions.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies face potential backpay liability for enhanced compensation rates applicable to nurses and healthcare workers, particularly for pandemic-era overtime and irregular work hours.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed the claim term 'encryption information' in a patent directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the Board's obviousness analysis was correct under that construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction of 'encryption information' in DivX's streaming media patent, vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded. The court adopted a narrower claim construction that could make the claims more vulnerable to prior art. This ruling is significant for the streaming media industry and the scope of patents covering digital rights management and encrypted content delivery.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming companies benefit from the potential narrowing or invalidation of DRM-related patents, reducing licensing exposure in the content delivery space.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the encrypted streaming and DRM technology space face risk that their claims will be construed more narrowly, increasing vulnerability to IPR challenges.
